text.title: Router Shadow Deploy — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every router / tuned-model shadow deploy must satisfy. Violating any one of these turns the shadow window into either noise or a stealth production change.

rule.statement: The candidate's response MUST be logged-only, never served to users. The traffic mirror returns the production router's response; the candidate's output is discarded after scoring.

rationale.source: canonical shadow-deploy pattern; Spotify / Stripe engineering blogs

rule.rationale: A shadow that occasionally leaks to users is no longer a shadow — it is an uncontrolled A/B with no statistical hygiene. The mirror must enforce response-discard at the network layer, not at the application's discretion.

rule.statement: Promotion requires per-request scoring delta (candidate vs production) to fall within the acceptance contract for ≥7 days of stable shadow traffic AND ≥1000 scored requests AND coverage across all intent classes.
rule.rationale: Less than 7 days hides weekday-cycle effects. Less than 1000 scored requests has insufficient power to detect small deltas. Missing an intent class promotes on the easy intents and explodes on the rare ones.

rule.statement: Promotion blocked if candidate's cost-per-request > baseline × 1.1 during the shadow window. Reductions are encouraged; spikes require explicit budget approval.

rationale.source: finops practice; LLM gateway routing literature

rule.rationale: Routers that look cheap can route to expensive models on the long tail of queries. The cost guardrail catches the routing-policy bug before production users see the bill. Even a 10% margin is a hard threshold without explicit signoff.

rule.statement: Response schema parity (key set, types, enum values) MUST be exactly 100% across the shadow window. Any divergence requires either rolling the schema first in production OR rejecting the candidate.
rule.rationale: Schema divergence in shadow looks harmless because users do not see candidate output. When promoted, downstream consumers (clients, mobile apps, analytics) break. The schema-first principle is non-negotiable.

rule.statement: Minimum shadow window: 7 days. Below 7 days, no promotion regardless of delta. Reason: model routing patterns vary across day-of-week and hour-of-day; smaller windows mis-estimate steady-state behaviour.

rationale.source: seasonality of LLM workload literature; production-deployment retrospectives

rule.rationale: A 24-hour shadow shows weekday traffic only; a 5-day window misses weekend behaviour. The 7-day floor is the cheapest insurance against the most-common over-confident promotion failure.

example.title: Successful shadow → promotion
example.code: Day 1: candidate router deployed in shadow at 100% mirror. 1.4M requests scored. Day 7: delta report: faithfulness +0.02 (within contract), cost -8% (better), schema parity 100%. Coverage: 3/3 intent classes within delta. Day 8: promotion gate passes. 5% traffic split (real cutover, not shadow) for 24h. Day 9: 25%. Day 10: 50%. Day 11: 100%. Old router decommissioned Day 14 after stable.
example.note: Illustrates r1-r5 fully satisfied. The 7-day window saw the weekend behaviour and the metric held.

example.title: The 24-hour shadow that broke production
example.code: Friday 10:00 — shadow deployed. Saturday 10:00 — "looks good, let's ship". Promoted Saturday afternoon. Monday morning — cost spike: candidate router was sending every "long-context" query to the expensive model, and Monday traffic has 4x more long-context queries than weekend. Cost +180%. Emergency rollback at noon.
example.note: r5 violation (24h window) and r3 not actually checked on representative traffic. The router was correct on weekend volume; the weekday workload was different.
